channel.title: Finance Time-Series Platform - Dev Journal
channel.description: Building an end-to-end data platform on Azure Databricks, one day at a time.

item.title: Days 5-10: Silver Features and a Star Schema in Gold
item.description:
What we built

Two full layers of the medallion architecture are now complete. Silver transforms raw OHLCV data into a rich feature set ready for machine learning. Gold introduces a proper star schema on top of it — dim_date, dim_ticker with SCD Type 2, and an insert-only fact table.

Silver layer (Days 5-7): feature engineering

The Silver layer is where raw price data becomes useful. Everything runs through features.py, a config-driven module that reads features.yaml — adding a new feature means editing the YAML, not touching the Python logic.

Cleaning: the illiquid day flag

Microcap stocks like HYFT regularly have days with zero trading volume — the stock simply didn’t trade. Rather than dropping these rows (which would break rolling window continuity), we flag them with an is_illiquid boolean column and keep them in the data.

One subtlety worth noting: ^VIX also has zero volume on every single row, but for a completely different reason — it’s an index, not a traded security, so the concept of “trading volume” doesn’t apply. We maintain a volume_exempt_tickers list in features.yaml to handle this case without special-casing it in the code.

Log returns

Rather than working with raw prices, we compute log returns: ln(price_today / price_yesterday). Two reasons:

Stationarity — raw prices trend up or down over time, which violates assumptions of most statistical and ML models. Log returns fluctuate around zero regardless of whether the stock is at $5 or $500.

Time additivity — log returns can be summed across time periods. Simple percentage returns cannot: a +100% gain followed by a -50% loss nets to zero, but 50% ≠ 0. Log returns handle this correctly.

The first row per ticker always has log_return = NULL — there’s no previous day to compare against. This is expected and correct.

Rolling volatility (20d and 60d)

Rolling volatility is the standard deviation of log returns over a backward-looking window. “Backward-looking” is the critical constraint: the window can only use past data, never future. Using future data here would be a look-ahead bias bug — the model would appear to predict the future because it was trained on it.

Both windows use Spark’s rowsBetween(-(N-1), 0) pattern, which is the clean way to express “current row plus the N-1 rows before it” in a window function. The 20-day window reacts quickly to recent volatility spikes; the 60-day window gives a smoother, longer-term picture. Both will feed into the clustering model as distinct signals.

We hit a Photon engine bug with 60-day windows that caused a RuntimeError: Incorrect sliding frame boundaries. The fix was straightforward — spark.conf.set("spark.databricks.photon.enabled", "false") disables the Photon accelerator for that computation. This is a known issue in certain Databricks runtime versions with larger sliding frames.

Moving averages (20, 50, 200)

Same rowsBetween window pattern, but with F.avg() instead of F.stddev(), applied to adj_close rather than log_return. The three windows correspond to roughly one month, one quarter, and one year of trading days — the standard technical analysis trio. Whether current price is above or below each moving average is a classic regime signal.

RSI

RSI (Relative Strength Index) is a momentum oscillator on a 0-100 scale. The calculation splits daily price changes into gains and losses, averages each over a 14-day window, and computes 100 - (100 / (1 + avg_gain/avg_loss)). Values above 70 are conventionally “overbought”, below 30 is “oversold”.

The one edge case worth handling: if avg_loss = 0 (all 14 days were gains), the formula would divide by zero. We return 100.0 in that case — pure-gain streaks saturate RSI at its maximum.

VIX correlation (60d)

VIX measures the market’s expected volatility over the next 30 days, derived from S&P 500 options prices. It’s commonly called the “fear index” — when markets panic, VIX spikes.

For our microcap universe, VIX correlation tells us whether a stock moves with market-wide sentiment or independently. A positive correlation means the stock tends to fall when fear rises (typical for speculative microcaps). A negative correlation is unusual — it suggests the stock reacts to company-specific news rather than macro sentiment.

Computing this required a self-join: since all tickers share one Silver table, we had to extract VIX returns as a separate DataFrame and join them back by trade_date before running the rolling F.corr() window.

NaN cleanup

After running all transformations, we found two rows with NaN (Not a Number) values — both for the current trading day, where adj_close wasn’t yet finalized by yfinance. NaN is different from NULL in Spark: isNull() won’t catch it, and it propagates silently through calculations. A final clean_nan() pass replaces all NaN values with NULL before writing to the Silver table.

Validation and tests (Day 8)

Before moving on, we validated the Silver table:

  • No NULL values in any column (confirmed — the sparse history for newer microcaps is just shorter, not missing)
  • Zero volume days correctly flagged as illiquid, VIX correctly exempt
  • NaN count across all feature columns: 0 after cleanup

We then wrote 10 pytest unit tests covering add_illiquid_flag(), add_log_returns(), and clean_nan(). Tests run locally against a local[1] SparkSession — no cluster connection needed, which keeps the feedback loop fast.

One thing worth noting about the test structure: the FeaturesConfig in tests is hardcoded, not read from features.yaml. This is intentional. Unit tests test isolated function logic, not the full pipeline including config loading. If a test read the YAML, changing a config value would break tests even when the function itself is perfectly correct — a false alarm.

Gold layer (Day 10): star schema

The Gold layer introduces dimensional modeling on top of Silver. Three tables, each with a specific role.

dim_date

A pre-generated calendar table covering 2015-2030, built once and never updated (the calendar doesn’t change). It’s a conformed dimension — shared across every fact table in the Gold layer, so that “quarter” or “month” always means exactly the same thing regardless of which fact table you’re querying.

The surrogate key is date_sk as an integer in YYYYMMDD format — a standard Kimball convention that makes date arithmetic readable without needing to join back to the dimension.

dim_ticker (SCD Type 2)

This dimension tracks when each ticker entered our tracked universe, and — as the SCD Type 2 tracked attribute — what market_cap_category it currently belongs to.

On each pipeline run, update_dim_ticker_scd2() compares the current category (derived from the latest valid closing price) against what’s stored. If it changed, the old row gets closed (effective_to = today, is_current = False) and a new current row gets inserted with a fresh surrogate key.

One implementation detail worth being explicit about: the “market cap category” here is derived from price alone, not from actual market cap (price × shares outstanding). This is a deliberate simplification — yfinance.history() doesn’t return shares outstanding, so we use price thresholds as a proxy. In a production system, you’d pull shares outstanding from a separate API endpoint. The SCD2 mechanics are identical regardless.

We also handle late-arriving dimensions via add_inferred_members(): if a fact row references a ticker that doesn’t yet exist in dim_ticker, a skeleton row gets inserted automatically rather than letting the fact become an orphan FK.

fact_ohlcv (insert-only)

The fact table at ticker × day grain. It joins to both dimensions via surrogate keys (ticker_sk, date_sk) and carries all Silver features as measures. Insert-only means once a trading day is recorded, it never changes — there’s no SCD logic on a fact table, only on dimensions.

After building the fact table, check_referential_integrity() runs a left_anti join to verify that every foreign key in the fact table has a matching row in its dimension. Both orphan counts came back at 0.

What’s next

Day 11 starts the ML work: K-means clustering over the feature set we’ve just built, with the goal of identifying distinct market regimes across the microcap universe.

item.title: Days 1-3: Foundations — Azure, Databricks, and Why This Project
item.description:
What I’m building, and why

This project is a finance time-series data platform built on Azure Databricks — daily OHLCV data for microcap AI stocks (including HYFT, which I actively follow), plus reference tickers SPY, QQQ, SMH, and VIX as a market-wide volatility signal.

The goal is twofold:

  1. A portfolio piece for Data/ML Engineer interviews, with a focus on time-series and finance/quant
  2. A real tool — a dashboard I’ll actually use myself to monitor my watchlist

The architecture follows the medallion pattern (Bronze → Silver → Gold), built as a config-driven framework rather than ad-hoc scripts — adding a new ticker or feature should mean editing a YAML file, not writing new code. Tech stack: Databricks Asset Bundles, Unity Catalog, PySpark, MLflow, and later Lakebase + Databricks Apps for serving and the dashboard.

Why finance/microcap and not, say, neuroscience (my original idea) or energy? Because I have a genuine personal interest in following these stocks, microcaps show more dramatic regime changes than blue-chips (visually and analytically richer data), and a finance degree gives me direct CV relevance. Productive struggle works best when you actually care about the outcome.

Days 1-2: Infrastructure

Before writing a single line of Python, I had to lay the groundwork:

  • Resource group + budget alerts (30/50/80/100%) — with a $200 starting credit, this is the first thing any serious cloud project needs
  • Automation Account with a managed identity as a safety net for auto-stopping resources, in case the alerts aren’t enough
  • Azure Databricks workspace (Premium tier) — Premium is mandatory, since only it supports Unity Catalog
  • Databricks CLI (the new, Go-based one — not the older databricks-cli pip package, which is a completely different tool; only the newer CLI supports bundle init)
  • GitHub repo + Databricks Asset Bundles (DAB) initialization

What are Databricks Asset Bundles, and why bother?

A Databricks Asset Bundle (DAB) is infrastructure-as-code for Databricks — it’s how you declare jobs, clusters, notebooks, pipelines, and permissions in YAML files that live in version control alongside your Python code. Running databricks bundle deploy pushes the entire declared state to the workspace; databricks bundle run <job> runs a specific job from that bundle.

The alternative is clicking through the Databricks UI to create jobs manually. That approach works for one-off exploration but fails immediately when you need:

  • Reproducibility — anyone who clones the repo and has credentials can bundle deploy and get an identical setup. No “works on my Databricks, not yours” problems.
  • Environment separation without duplication — a single databricks.yml file declares both dev and prod targets. The variables block parameterizes the differences (catalog name, cluster size, etc.). There’s no separate prod copy of job definitions to keep in sync.
  • Dev mode resource prefixing — when you deploy to the dev target, Databricks automatically prepends [dev <username>] to every resource name. You can have five developers working in the same workspace without their jobs or tables colliding. In prod mode, names are exactly as declared.
  • Git-native CI/CD — because the bundle is files, a GitHub Action can call databricks bundle deploy --target prod on every merge to main. The deployment pipeline is just another shell command.
  • Rollback — if a production job breaks, you revert the commit and redeploy. The previous state is in git history.

The practical consequence: the jobs I’ll build for the Bronze → Silver → Gold pipeline will be defined in the bundle from day one. Adding a new ticker or a new transformation step means editing a YAML config and redeploying — not clicking through the UI and hoping you replicated everything correctly.

Key realization: Unity Catalog is the default now

I expected to manually set up a metastore through the Databricks Account Console. Turned out that wasn’t needed at all — Unity Catalog is automatically enabled for every workspace created after November 2023. I just opened the Catalog panel in the workspace, and it was already there.

A good reminder that documentation and tutorials go stale fast — what was a required manual step last year, the platform now handles on its own.

Day 3: DAB configuration, cluster, and dev/prod separation

Why one workspace, not two

I considered whether dev and prod needed two separate Databricks workspaces. Decision: no — one workspace is enough, and I separate environments through Unity Catalog catalogs (finance_dev vs finance_prod). For a solo portfolio project, a second workspace would just double the cost with no real benefit. This is a deliberate architectural choice, not a shortcut — and that’s exactly how I’ll defend it in an interview.

My databricks.yml now has two targets:

targets:
  dev:
    mode: development
    variables:
      catalog: finance_dev
  prod:
    mode: production
    variables:
      catalog: finance_prod
    permissions:
      - user_name: azuredominik007@seznam.cz
        level: CAN_MANAGE

One detail worth noting: in dev mode, Databricks automatically prefixes every resource ([dev username]) and sets you as the owner, so an explicit permissions block isn’t needed there. In prod mode it’s the opposite — nothing is implicit, so access has to be granted explicitly.

Interactive cluster vs. job cluster

Another distinction I clarified today: an interactive cluster (for notebook development) is created manually in the UI and keeps running until you stop it. A job cluster, on the other hand, is defined directly in the DAB bundle — it spins up automatically when a job runs and terminates when it’s done. That will be the standard for the production pipeline (bronze→silver→gold). Today I only set up the former, for development.

Side project: a dev blog

I decided to keep a running blog about building this project — Quarto + GitHub Pages, living right inside the repo. It’s not just a portfolio nicety: writing in my own words about what I did today and why is the best form of retrieval practice I know. The blog will run alongside the project for the full 100 days.

Why Quarto, specifically

A Quarto blog sits in a sweet spot that most alternatives don’t reach. Here’s why it won this decision:

It’s built for technical writing with code. Quarto is a literate programming environment — you write prose and code in the same .qmd file, execute the code, and the output (tables, charts, prints) is embedded directly in the rendered HTML. No copy-pasting a plot from a notebook into a post. If I run a backtest or build a feature importance chart on day 40, I’ll include the actual live-executed result, not a screenshot.

One source, many outputs. The same .qmd file can render to HTML (the blog), a PDF report, or even a slide deck — just by changing the format in the header. This matters when the project matures: analysis written for the blog can be repurposed into a portfolio PDF without rewriting anything.

GitHub Pages deployment is genuinely trivial. Setting output-dir: docs in _quarto.yml means quarto render produces a static site directly into the docs/ folder. GitHub Pages serves from docs/ on the main branch — no CI pipeline, no build server, no Action required. One command, push, done.

Asset bundling out of the box. When Quarto renders the blog, it bundles all JavaScript and CSS dependencies (Bootstrap, highlight.js, MathJax, search index) into the docs/site_libs/ folder alongside the HTML. The entire site is self-contained — there’s no CDN dependency at runtime. The reader gets the same experience whether they’re online or viewing a local copy. For a portfolio project that needs to be reliably accessible for interview reviewers, this is not a minor point.

Search, RSS feed, and categories for free. The index.qmd listing page already has feed: true — that’s a full Atom/RSS feed at /index.xml with zero extra work. The categories: true key on the listing generates a tag system automatically. Quarto also generates a search.json and wires up client-side full-text search across all posts, again with no configuration beyond what’s already in the repo.

Math rendering. Finance and ML writing regularly needs notation — returns, covariance matrices, loss functions. Quarto renders LaTeX inline and in display blocks via MathJax. Writing is a one-liner in the source.

The alternative I seriously considered was a standard Jekyll or Hugo site. Both are fine, but they require separating the notebook work from the blog writing — you’d run the analysis in a notebook, export results, then write about them in a separate Markdown file. Quarto collapses that into one step, which for a data engineering project with a lot of code is the right call.

What’s next

Day 4 is about storage — a Unity Catalog external location backed by ADLS Gen2, and the first real Bronze Delta table. That’s where I finally get to the data.

item.title: Post With Code
item.dc:creator: Harlow Malloc
item.description: This is a post with executable code.

item.title: Welcome To My Blog
item.dc:creator: Tristan O'Malley
item.description:
This is the first post in a Quarto blog. Welcome!

Since this post doesn’t specify an explicit image, the first image in the post will be used in the listing page of posts.
